---
title: "Super PI Ubuntu - How fast is your cpu?"
date: 2005-08-27
forum: The Cafe
---

### Post by sapo on 2005-08-27
SuperPi is a quick and easy way to measure processor / chipset performance.

You can Download the linux version here:

[ftp://pi.super-computing.org/Linux/super_pi.tar.gz](ftp://pi.super-computing.org/Linux/super_pi.tar.gz)

Just untar and run the command as:

```
./super_pi 20
```

Then post a screenshot of your results or paste it  :-P 

I didnt know that there was a superpi for linux, i used to use it in windows to test my overclock.. my new cpu arrived today and i tested my first overlclock:

A64 2800+ @ 2ghz s754 + Mobo Epox 8KRDA3J Running Hoary x86

[img]http://img376.imageshack.us/img376/7343/superpi7dx.jpg[/img]

Please post your score  :grin:

### Post by skoal on 2005-09-19
End of calculation.    Time=      62.937 Sec.
 End of data output.    Time=       0.300 Sec.
 Total calculation(I/O) time=      63.237(       3.665) Sec.

on P4-1.7GoogleHertz...

By the way, did you know that PI is *exactly* 3.0? Any great chef will tell you that.  They only use the mathematical value when calculating the ingredients for a cherry PI dish, leaving you the tasty dish crumbs as the *remainder*, and also as a *reminder*...

\\//_

---

### Post by benplaut on 2005-09-19
[QUOTE=skoal]By the way, did you know that PI is *exactly* 3.0? Any great chef will tell you that.  They only use the mathematical value when calculating the ingredients for a cherry PI dish, leaving you the tasty dish crumbs as the *remainder*, and also as a *reminder*...

\\//_[/QUOTE]

yes, it's common knowledge, but the mathmaticians don't like to admit it  :wink:

### Post by futz on 2006-11-29
AMD Athlon 64 X2 4200+, DFI LANPARTY UT nF4 Ultra-D, 1GB RAM
Very small overclock. This CPU is basically two 3500+ cores on a die, so I come in neck and neck with the 3500+ number I see near the top of the list. Just a tiny bit faster because of the very mild overclock. It's capable of a LOT more, but it needs better than stock cooling to get there (and survive for long) and I haven't gotten around to doing that yet.

```

Woo! I think that beats out everyone who's posted so far, and I know my processor's by no means the fastest in existence.

O.M.G....
Almost a half than my cpu (27secs)

### Post by jdong on 2006-12-03
Yes, SuperPI is a single-threaded benchmark. It also tends to produce dramatically better results on the Intel Pentium M and Intel Core architectures than on AMD's competing products, even if in other benchmarks the two processors score similarly.

---

 I=10 L=     174        Time=       0.808 Sec.
 I=11 L=     349        Time=       0.804 Sec.
 I=12 L=     698        Time=       0.804 Sec.
 I=13 L=    1396        Time=       0.800 Sec.
 I=14 L=    2794        Time=       0.800 Sec.
 I=15 L=    5588        Time=       0.800 Sec.
 I=16 L=   11176        Time=       0.792 Sec.
 I=17 L=   22353        Time=       0.780 Sec.
 I=18 L=   44707        Time=       0.756 Sec.
 I=19 L=   89415        Time=       0.700 Sec.
 End of main loop
 End of calculation.    Time=      16.229 Sec.
 End of data output.    Time=       0.104 Sec.
** Total calculation(I/O) time=      16.333(       0.636) Sec.**
 ------ Ended super_pi run : Thu Nov 30 00:22:33 GMT 2006
```

Woo! I think that beats out everyone who's posted so far, and I know my processor's by no means the fastest in existence.

I think "fastest" now is a relative term. The new Core 2 Duo architecture is a vast improvement over both the Pentium 4, Pentium D and Athlon architectures as most of the current benchmarks will show. A 2.0Ghz Core 2 Duo will usually outperform a Pentium D 940 3.2ghz CPU like mine. The difference is that Core no longer uses Netburst and both cores can share the same cache memory without going through the Front Side Bus. The Front Side Bus speed is also 1066mhz which is much faster than the Pentium D clas s of cpu. Intel has really outdone themselves with the Core 2 series. I'm eagerly waiting to see what AMD comes up with next to top them as they have tended to be a tad more innovative than Intel in the past several years. Ain't competition a great thing?

---

### Post by jdong on 2007-01-05
> **macogw said:**
> 

Fluxbox and WindowMaker are still faster than not having X at all...WEIRD

I don't know how to overclock.  How does this work?
Probably pure coincidence. Run it in single-user recovery mode and that should attain the fastest possible results :)

Don't overclock if you don't know how. That's a pretty good sign not to attempt it. In reality, it's nowadays a BIOS option on BIOS'es that support overclocking. Unless you know what you're doing, you can g et yourself into some serious trouble, like hangs, infinite reboot loops, and inability to make it back into the BIOS (all at least requiring resetting the CMOS) so be careful :)

---

### Post by macogw on 2007-01-05
> **jdong said:**
> Probably pure coincidence. Run it in single-user recovery mode and that should attain the fastest possible results :)

Don't overclock if you don't know how. That's a pretty good sign not to attempt it. In reality, it's nowadays a BIOS option on BIOS'es that support overclocking. Unless you know what you're doing, you can g et yourself into some serious trouble, like hangs, infinite reboot loops, and inability to make it back into the BIOS (all at least requiring resetting the CMOS) so be careful :)
Yeah, I found out what I did wrong.  ctrl alt f1 doesn't kill X, just makes it not visible.  Should've typed sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop ....so I'll try that later and fix my post accordingly

### Post by ~LoKe on 2007-02-07
> @ ~Loke: Am I reading your post right? Is that 6300 overclocked to 3.36Ghz? That's like double stock. Is it hard to keep cool or are the core 2 really that efficient?
Yeah, my E6300 is OC'd to 3.36.  480x7.  My computer is water cooled, and the load temps are still in the mid/high 50's.  I'm stress testing as we speak and it's not liking my OC too much.  Had to pump up the voltage to get Orthos to stop failing me.  We'll see how it goes.

Eventually I'd like to get it to to 500FSB (3.5GHz), but that's not too realistic right now.

**EDIT:** And yeah, that's a 90% overclock.

---

### Post by m.musashi on 2007-02-07
> **Choad said:**
> whats the factor that makes my core 2 duo faster than yours? its the 2 ghz model, same as urs

i would have thought they'd perform the same.... but theres 4 seconds in it. im not trying to gloat btw, i am genuinely interested

I'm not sure but I have the core duo and you the core 2 duo. One difference is the larger L2 cache. I think the core 2 has 4 mb and the core just 2 mb. I'm not sure if that has much impact on this test or not though. There could be configuration issues too. I have a pretty stock edgy install with no attempt made at speeding anything up. Mine is a dell and I suppose there could be issues with the mobo used too but I'm not sure. Maybe dell uses cheap parts - wouldn't suprise me.

However, you would think they would be similar so maybe it's just the L2 bit.

> **~LoKe said:**
> Yeah, my E6300 is OC'd to 3.36.  480x7.  My computer is water cooled, and the load temps are still in the mid/high 50's.  I'm stress testing as we speak and it's not liking my OC too much.  Had to pump up the voltage to get Orthos to stop failing me.  We'll see how it goes.

Eventually I'd like to get it to to 500FSB (3.5GHz), but that's not too realistic right now.

**EDIT:** And yeah, that's a 90% overclock.
Damn! I don't know much about overclocking but Damn. However, other than smoking all of us on the speed test, does that overclock buy you anything day to day? I suppose if you are a serious gamer it would but for me my cpu sits at 1-2% just about all the time. I can't imagine an overclock would help me at all. In fact, the 4800 I have is pretty much going to waste. I can rip and burn a DVD in about 20 minutes or so but other than that I am not tapping the power that I have. I did just start folding@home though so maybe I'm giving a bit back to the world now.

---

On AT i used to be Klixxer, maybe before your time but me and n0c reigned in OS and OT during night time for a while.

Other than that, yeah, except for number crunching, any overclock besides a bus speed overclock is mostly for show.

Hey everyone!  I've been using Ubuntu since Warty (and Debian 2.0 before that), and have long since been poking around the forums.  Thought I would make my first post and pop the cherry by sharing my super_pi 1M run with you.  Just got this new machine around Christmas time and am really liking it.

Specs are:
M/B: Asus P5W-DH Deluxe, rev. 1.04g
Proc: Core 2 Duo E6600 (Week 28, B stepping, fsb @ 400 Mhz x 9 multi.)
Memory: 2GB Corsair Dominator 6400C4, DDR2-800
Gpu: XFX 7900GS (at 600/720)

This run was done with Beryl, Firefox, and Matlab open (one core was completely taken crunching away at a project).  Total memory usage was at ~350 Mb.

I have also followed a couple threads for Windows SuperPi 1M postings, and have found that Linux SuperPi times for the same system can be significantly lower than for SuperPi on Windows.  Anybody know why that may be?  Some forum threads won't let users post Linux times because they claim an unfair advantage.  I say its more than fair and just feel bad for them because they are using Windows :biggrin:

My system has an intel core 2 duo E6600 with 2gb ram.  Here is what my system got while running amarok beryl firefox gnome etc. also
```
 ------ Started super_pi run : Sun Feb 25 20:19:53 EST 2007
 Start of PI calculation up to 1048576 decimal digits
 End of initialization. Time=       0.252 Sec.
 I= 1 L=       0        Time=       0.584 Sec.
 I= 2 L=       0        Time=       0.684 Sec.
 I= 3 L=       1        Time=       0.696 Sec.
 I= 4 L=       2        Time=       0.676 Sec.
 I= 5 L=       5        Time=       0.676 Sec.
 I= 6 L=      10        Time=       0.672 Sec.
 I= 7 L=      21        Time=       0.672 Sec.
 I= 8 L=      43        Time=       0.672 Sec.
 I= 9 L=      87        Time=       0.672 Sec.
 I=10 L=     174        Time=       0.672 Sec.
 I=11 L=     349        Time=       0.672 Sec.
 I=12 L=     698        Time=       0.668 Sec.
 I=13 L=    1396        Time=       0.672 Sec.
 I=14 L=    2794        Time=       0.676 Sec.
 I=15 L=    5588        Time=       0.664 Sec.
 I=16 L=   11176        Time=       0.664 Sec.
 I=17 L=   22353        Time=       0.652 Sec.
 I=18 L=   44707        Time=       0.636 Sec.
 I=19 L=   89415        Time=       0.588 Sec.
 End of main loop
 End of calculation.    Time=      13.357 Sec.
 End of data output.    Time=       0.072 Sec.
 Total calculation(I/O) time=      13.429(       1.972) Sec.

```
Furthermore, when I ran two of them simultaneously, the scores were about 15 or 16 seconds for each of them.  Ohh the power of dual core.

Another interesting thing to point out is that while running in wine (I downloaded the .exe) I recorded a score of 23 seconds, significantly slower than the 13 I got before.  A slow down was expected though as it was running in an emulator.

I continued my experimenting by running it on my brother's machine of similar specs but running windows instead of linux.  His system has the latest nvidia northbridge wheras mine has the intel ich8 and his system has 800 ram while mine only has 667.  His system took 30 seconds, which is slower than both linux and linux with wine.  I personally found these results to be pretty amazing.

### Post by bward1 on 2007-03-04
I'm not overclocking at all.  I'm guessing that the difference between our performance is due to the differing L2 cache sizes.  While granted your processor is running a little faster than mine is, mine has twice the L2 cache, so that may make up the difference.  I'm not entirely sure how the L2 cache works, but I know that bigger is better, and that is the only thing I can think of that would cause the differing performance.

### Post by m.musashi on 2007-04-17
> **makinasvp said:**
> Here are my results...
Are they any good?

Good is kind of subjective but those are not bad numbers. The fastest I've seen here is about 13-14 seconds for over-clocked core 2s. Some are well into 1 minute + so yours are definitely in the top half or better. I can't remember but my 4800 X2 was in the 24 sec range and I'm pretty happy with my set up.

EDIT: oops, 29 secs.

My Thinkpad X31 (Pentium M 1.4GHz 1MB L2 cache, 768MB RAM)

I have my Toshiba's result (Pentium M 760 2.0GHz, 2MB L2 cache) somewhere around here, too.

End of calculation.    Time=      31.186 Sec.
 End of data output.    Time=       0.188 Sec.
 Total calculation(I/O) time=      31.374(       0.820) Sec.
 ------ Ended super_pi run : Thu Nov 30 11:37:26 WET 2006

That was back in November, I suppose I can get a better result now.Damn, my Thinkpad X31's result was with Debian Etch... I managed to shave 3 seconds out of it with Ubuntu Feisty. I say it's strange, because that result was with Debian Etch and the i686 kernel, and Ubuntu's result was with a generic kernel image.

Nice. When I ran my E6600 at 3GHz I got 12.125s. You must be running a quite tweaked ubuntu or something? I laugh at the overclocking team that just broke the 9s limit of calculating 1M with superpi! They used a Intel Core 2 Duo X6800 running at 5620 MHz and they got 8,985s. In Linux that record would have been around 4seconds better or more.

I ran superpi in Vista 1M and got 16s at 3GHz, in Linux 12,125s. Although I only tried it once, it seems to vary up and down in Linux. :p

### Post by grndslm on 2007-09-03
> **christhemonkey said:**
> Mine looks very slow after that one!
...
```
Total calculation(I/O) time=      52.703(       1.928) Sec.
```
...

This is with a AMD Athlonx XP 2600+ 2.1GhzYes, it does look very slow doesn't it?  =^p

This single-threaded app shows that even just one core from a C2D or quad-core chip are super efficient!!  One 3.4GHz does the work in about 4.5 times as fast as this Athlon.  Could you imagine apps like these being multi-threaded?!?!? \/\/0\/\/!

I'm really not pickin', I'm just sayin'... this thing is fast!

### Post by grndslm on 2007-09-03

> It normally runs at 2200MHz... but my main man Deven Gallo gave me some tips and pointers, and I now have it running at about 2500MHz.

### Post by PsycoGeek on 2007-09-10
You know, these numbers are all well and good for the ego, but what really matters is how usable the system is to _YOU_.  Yeah, faster is better for everyone, but if the system makes you happy with the way it runs, the numbers don't mean a thing...

### Post by DeaconJones on 2007-09-14

```

./super_pi 20
```

### Post by najames on 2007-09-15
This was really strange.  I ran Super PI, with a browser open, radio playing.  It yielded times at about 35sec on my AMD 3600+ running at about 2Ghz.  Then I opened system monitor while it was running and noticed a really funky sine wave CPU usage pattern.  Is this common with Intel processors too?  Curious, I expected one of the cores to be more flat lined while Super PI was running.

### Post by najames on 2007-09-16
"So what does this even tell me other than other people are faster, and some slower?"

I used it for tweaking, testing before/after BIOS updates, comparing different processors, comparing while changing overclocking settings.  I usually undervolt the CPUs so they run cooler and Super PI is a quick and dirty stability test to see if it will work at current settings.

When I build a PC, I usually install a WinXP SP2 disk do a lot of tweaking and benchmarking because more "common benchmarks" are available for WInBlows platforms. I also usually flash the BIOS at this time.  After I am satisfied, I usually wipe it and install Ubuntu.  Sorry Bill Gates.

It is interesting that this 3600+ Brisbane @2.0Ghz with Feisty using a newer Gutsy kernel turns in the same times as my 5000+ Brisbane with WinXP/Super Pi @ 2.6Ghz did out of the box.

Curious, I expected one of the cores to be more flat lined while Super PI was running.

You can see where it switched cores in mid-stream. Don't know what it means, or what's supposed to be "normal"...

### Post by toupeiro on 2007-09-19
Did a little reading on super pi to make sure I am getting good info from a dual core system.  Results are as expected.  Better performance when both cores are sharing affinity of a single super-pi process than when I am running a super_pi process per core.  
 
CPU: Athlon 64 X2 3800+ 2GB RAM running dual channel DDR-200 speed (hey, it was cheap!)

Temperature: unsure.  haven't setup any of that on this box before..  Guess I should.  it never gets shut down and BIOS has always reported it within average operating temperature on CPU and Ambient temp.  I have no reason to suspect it being any different.

Compiz-Fusion
PostGRESQL 8.1
Firefox

### Post by mocha on 2007-10-23
The last 3 posts with the 6750 and quad 6600 should be totally blowing everyone else away.  Did you guys try overclocking?  Do those processors overclock as well as the older 63x0 and 64x0 models?  I easily reached 3 GHz on my 6420 with air cooling.  Over 3 GHz it won't boot.  It runs 24/7 since June.  No instability or overheating problems whatsoever.

### Post by jgcamp99 on 2007-10-29
> **J33pG33k said:**
> I have also followed a couple threads for Windows SuperPi 1M postings, and have found that Linux SuperPi times for the same system can be significantly lower than for SuperPi on Windows.  Anybody know why that may be?  Some forum threads won't let users post Linux times because they claim an unfair advantage.  I say its more than fair and just feel bad for them because they are using Windows :biggrin:

I like the way you think ! I have nothing against Windows per say, but my Linux Super Pi 1M's are a good 7 seconds faster with Ubuntu 7.04, Beryl and Firefox open /runnning, also running lmsensors too. What impresses me about Linux, you can have the eye candy running and get better times, meanwhile, with Windows, you have scores where processes and anything that's potential OH has to be shut down/killed and it's that 7 seconds or more slower.

I'd be curious, anyone run SuperPi 1M on Vista Ultimate and fully loaded/bloated  ?

---

 End of main loop

```

**WITHOUT X:** 14.041 Sec.
I thought it would make much more of a difference.

**WINDOWS XP PRO SP2 (fresh install):** 17.729 Sec.
w00t, down with Microsoft!

### Post by stlcoptony on 2007-12-05
not to be picky, but does having this quad core actually make a difference - I see above my post that someone with a core 2 duo got almost the same times. Does ubuntu recognize that there is 4 processors?

thanks

### Post by ~LoKe on 2007-12-06
> **stlcoptony said:**
> not to be picky, but does having this quad core actually make a difference - I see above my post that someone with a core 2 duo got almost the same times. Does ubuntu recognize that there is 4 processors?

thanks

Super Pi only uses one core, so a 3.6GHz Core 2 Quad and a 3.6 GHz Core 2 Duo could have roughly the same times.

### Post by Presto123 on 2007-12-06
> **~LoKe said:**
> Super Pi only uses one core, so a 3.6GHz Core 2 Quad and a 3.6 GHz Core 2 Duo could have roughly the same times.

I wonder...does it make a difference which one it uses? It seems that certain processes are handled by one core and others by the other core.

I think my computer is seriously slow compared to the other dual-core people here. I just bought the thing about 2 months ago and it is a AMD Athlon 64 x2 4000+ running on an MSI mobo. (Package deal from Tiger Direct)

---

### Post by ~LoKe on 2007-12-06
> **Presto123 said:**
> I wonder...does it make a difference which one it uses? It seems that certain processes are handled by one core and others by the other core.

I think my computer is seriously slow compared to the other dual-core people here. I just bought the thing about 2 months ago and it is a AMD Athlon 64 x2 4000+ running on an MSI mobo. (Package deal from Tiger Direct)

Package deals are usually bad news, and AMD is really only suggested for people on a budget/cheap HTPC.  A 2.4GHz AMD X2 and a 2.4GHz Core2Duo will be significantly different, so make sure you're comparing your results with other processors from the same family.

And no, it shouldn't matter which core is used.  They should all be the same speed, and if they aren't, there's a real serious problem. ;)  The only difference between two or more cores is the temperature.  One might run hotter than the other because of how the thermal paste is applied.

---

### Post by roaldz on 2007-12-08
> **~LoKe said:**
> Package deals are usually bad news, and AMD is really only suggested for people on a budget/cheap HTPC.  A 2.4GHz AMD X2 and a 2.4GHz Core2Duo will be significantly different, so make sure you're comparing your results with other processors from the same family.

And no, it shouldn't matter which core is used.  They should all be the same speed, and if they aren't, there's a real serious problem. ;)  The only difference between two or more cores is the temperature.  One might run hotter than the other because of how the thermal paste is applied.

Well, that difference in temperature is SO minimal, you don´t have to worry about that one.. I´d rather make sure you HAVE thermal paste:)

---

For 25:

Now that I have 33.5 million places for PI, how does that help me determine how much dough I need for a 9" diameter pie?

I had an old Texas Instruments TI-59, with the cheesy magnetic card readers.  It took 24 hours to calculate pi to several places (I forget how many).  The results were stored in registers that you had to manually cycle through.  Well, at least we got to the moon with slide rules.

### Post by ~LoKe on 2008-01-15
> **zeno0771 said:**
> This is getting to be kind of a drag...Most overclocker forums won't let me play because I run Linux, most Linux forums won't let me play because I OC, and no one else running Fedora that I know of does either.  Soooo, from what I've seen, people in the Ubuntu forums are nicer than most so how about cutting an overachieving machine a break?

You stole the [record from me](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=3813568&postcount=351) by about 0.02, lol.  Damn you!

---

### Post by zeno0771 on 2008-01-16
> **~LoKe said:**
> You stole the [record from me](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=3813568&postcount=351) by about 0.02, lol.  Damn you!
.02, eh?
Here's a hint:  you'll almost always get a better time on a fresh start; stick an obviously-named text file on your desktop to remind you to run 1M next time you have to restart.  You could put it in rc.local I suppose but I don't know if it wouldn't be competing for CPU cycles then.
If it makes you feel any better, that wasn't my best time, just the best time that I got a screenie for:cool::cool:

---

### Post by ahaslam on 2008-01-16
Just wondering what load your Q6600's are under for this? My E6300 takes 3 secs more than you guys. It uses 1 core at 100%, I suspect you're in the same boat?

PS. 0.02sec... 10x that can be gained or lost depending on distro alone ;)

---

### Post by zeno0771 on 2008-01-16
> **ahaslam said:**
> Just wondering what load your Q6600's are under for this? My E6300 takes 3 secs more than you guys. It uses 1 core at 100%, I suspect you're in the same boat?

PS. 0.02sec... 10x that can be gained or lost depending on distro alone ;)

Funny you should say that...My last CPU was a 6300...did 15 and change on 2.96GHz.
On mine GKrellm shows 100% on one core for a little more than the blink of an eye.  The thing with the 1M test is that it fits entirely in L2 cache; earlier I told ~LoKe about running Pi after a restart; the cache shouldn't be too full of instructions for other running processes.

---

### Post by Gestalt73 on 2008-01-28
> **zeno0771 said:**
> *Only* 4 GHz on a Yorkie??
Gestalt, what kind of cooling are you using?  I've seen these things break 5 GHz on water...

I know, right?  That's the whole reason I bought the chip.  It's running watercooled right now @ 1.6000v.
temps:
[LIST]
[*]idle 88 fahrenheit (31 celsius)
[*]load 122 fahrenheit (50 celsius)
[/LIST]

But I either have a bum chip or there's some configuration left to do.  It's a week 39.  I also couldn't push the board FSB as high as most others, it tops out at about 366Mhz.  I'm ordering some different memory to see if it helps.  Some other things possibly working against me are that I'm running 64 bit and it's a quad which means that I can generate twice as much heat under full load than a duallie.

The other thing to keep in mind  is that many of these review sites are running engineering samples, which may have been hand picked to highlight the overclockability of the platform.

I'd love to turn in some 7.5s SuperPI at 5Ghz.

### Post by Scarath on 2008-02-02
I have some questions about Super Pi that i cant find the answers to anywhere.

1) why are modern laptops (apart from the old ones) often faster than desktops when they have lower Ghz? e.g. i have seen laptops be faster than a desktop even tho the lappy has a 'slower' CPU.
2) Does Super Pi take advantage of multi cores?
3) Does running a 32/64 bit OS make any difference?

I have seen it in this thread but here is my personal example:

Desktop: I have a AMD 64 running at 2.2Ghz on 34 bit Debian and it makes 38 seconds
Laptop: My gf has a intel celeron M (core duo) running at 1.8 Ghz and it makes 27 seconds

both computers have a gig of ram.

thanks

---

### Post by ~LoKe on 2008-02-02
> **Scarath said:**
> 1) why are modern laptops (apart from the old ones) often faster than desktops when they have lower Ghz? e.g. i have seen laptops be faster than a desktop even tho the lappy has a 'slower' CPU.
It depends.  A 2.2GHz AMD X2 is slower than a 2.2GHz Core 2 Duo.  The numbers don't really mean much, they measure them differently.  And GHz isn't everything.

> **Scarath said:**
> 
2) Does Super Pi take advantage of multi cores?
Super Pi is a single threaded application, so one instance uses only one core[/quote]

> **Scarath said:**
> 
3) Does running a 32/64 bit OS make any difference?
I'm pretty sure Super Pi is a 32bit app, so no, it shouldn't make a difference (though if it did, it would be minute).

---

### Post by roaldz on 2008-02-04
> **~LoKe said:**
> It depends.  A 2.2GHz AMD X2 is slower than a 2.2GHz Core 2 Duo.  The numbers don't really mean much, they measure them differently.  And GHz isn't everything.

Well, benchmarking with a program like "superpi" is not accurate enough. Who calculates pi all the time? If you really want to put your system to the test, run lmbench or something.
I guess it's better to put that in another thread.

[apt:lmbench]("apt:lmbench")

---

> Well, benchmarking with a program like "superpi" is not accurate enough. Who calculates pi all the time? If you really want to put your system to the test, run lmbench or something.
I guess it's better to put that in another thread.

[apt:lmbench]("apt:lmbench")

Super_Pi is also cross-platform and easy to configure.  Of course Windows users will complain about Linux having an "unfair advantage" but you can't win 'em all, right?:mrgreen:
Think of it as drag-racing for CPUs...

### Post by Pap3r on 2008-03-06
Athlon X2 4600+ Manchester (Worst OC'ing model EVER) at 2691Mhz.  I've pushed it up to 2829Mhz, unstable though.  Could probably put it at 2900, but it would be 100% unstable :)

Question though, how does this translate into windows superpi?  I get 32seconds in windows 1M SuperPi.  This got me 28s, so I assume it's not the same test.  How can I compare my linux speeds vs windows speeds?  Plus, the 32s in windoz was my best, with an OC 120~Mhz higher than linux.

### Post by yowshi on 2008-03-08
[QUOTE=Pap3r;4467796]Athlon X2 4600+ Manchester (Worst OC'ing model EVER) at 2691Mhz.  I've pushed it up to 2829Mhz, unstable though.  Could probably put it at 2900, but it would be 100% unstable :)

Question though, how does this translate into windows superpi?  I get 32seconds in windows 1M SuperPi.  This got me 28s, so I assume it's not the same test.  How can I compare my linux speeds vs windows speeds?  Plus, the 32s in windoz was my best, with an OC 120~Mhz higher than linux.

Seen here: [http://www.overclock.net/attachments/benchmarking-software-discussion/65959d1203034776-official-superpi-1m-top-times-superpi.png](http://www.overclock.net/attachments/benchmarking-software-discussion/65959d1203034776-official-superpi-1m-top-times-superpi.png)
Anyone know?
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

it probably is from what i understand linux doesnt have the same amount of background crap using up cpu cycles as windows does so the 4 extra seconds could be from that

### Post by farruinn on 2008-03-10
> **yowshi said:**
> Question though, how does this translate into windows superpi?  I get 32seconds in windows 1M SuperPi.  This got me 28s, so I assume it's not the same test.  How can I compare my linux speeds vs windows speeds?  Plus, the 32s in windoz was my best, with an OC 120~Mhz higher than linux.

It's probably the same test (ie, mostly the same code) but the program still has to use the underlying operating system and libraries for things like memory allocation and whatnot. Also, your default Linux install might have less crap running than your windows environment. I doubt there is a way to convert your "linux time" into "windows time" like you would "dog years" to "human years" since it depends on many variables. I would just be happy to accept that Linux is more efficient :)

---

### Post by GTengineer on 2008-03-25
> **ahaslam said:**
> GTengineer, nice OC, is that water cooled?

yep

Swifteck Apogee GT water block with MCR320 triple fan radiator (3 fans push air and 3 on the other side pulling). The radiator is overkill because the ambient temperatures in my room are never cold enough anyway. But it keeps it cool even at 50% fan speeds.

Nice OC yourself on that 6300. I had a E4300 before but I could not get it over 3.4GHz even on water.

### Post by roaldz on 2008-04-04
> **ellalan said:**

Open a terminal (applications->accessoires->terminal *or* ALT+F2) and go to the super_pi dir:
```
cd /home/ellalans_user_name/path/to/super_pi
```
and run
```
./super_pi 20
```

This should give you the output.

### Post by roots on 2008-04-06
> **roaldz said:**
> 
[QUOTE=ellalans]
Hi roaldz
I downloaded pi.tar.gz to my desktop,extracted and ran in Terminal. then I got some readings like others but when it came to "End of mainloop" all of them vanished,anyway I wouldn't know how to copy this to the post.I am a slow learner so you have to bear with me. For your information, my Initialization Time was=2.36sec and the rest of the times were something in the region of 10.23secs. That is how I came to the conclusion that mine was slow.

Assuming you are using gnome (ubuntu default):
Open a terminal (applications->accessoires->terminal *or* ALT+F2) and go to the super_pi dir:
```
cd /home/ellalans_user_name/path/to/super_pi
```
and run
```
./super_pi 20
```

This should give you the output.[/QUOTE]

as i've understood his problem is not executing super_pi but some kind of strange things happening while running it.

ellalans, if your terminal doesn't keep the output, try

```

./super_pi 20 > results.txt

```

which should write the output to the (txt) file given after ">". the file does not need to exist before, but you should know that by directing the output to a file, you will get *no* output in the terminal.

as for the problem occured: i can't help you with that as i haven't used super_pi before. my suggestions would be: check you cpu load/temps while running it - one core should be running on 100%. are your cpu temps fine? check if there's any buglist or forums on super_pi....


cheers,
roots

---

one more note: both cat /proc/cpuinfo and sysinfo show wrong cpu clocks (at least for E8400 and E8500), which seems be due to some "misinterpretion" of the cpu multiplier.


.roots

### Post by GTengineer on 2008-04-06
> **roots said:**
> what's also strange, results-wise...with a plus of 1GHz above the E8400@3000 some posts earlier, my E8500@4008 is only gaining about 1.2 seconds...but hell yeah, i can say: "look, my d**k is 0.7mm longer!!!!" :lolflag:

one more note: both cat /proc/cpuinfo and sysinfo show wrong cpu clocks (at least for E8400 and E8500), which seems be due to some "misinterpretion" of the cpu multiplier.


.roots

It is not a misinterpretation these CPU's have a feature in the BIOS called speedstep which lowers the multiplier when not working or very little load to reduce the clock speed, which also reduces heat produced and power consumption. If you disable it in the BIOS it will show always the correct clock speed but it is better leaving it on.

If you run cat /proc/cpuinfo while running a CPU intensive task it will show the proper clockspeed even if speedstep it on.

---

### Post by roots on 2008-04-06
i know, but that's a different thing. i have speedstep disabled in bios for this kind of testing business. furthermore, the clockings shown by - for example - cat /proc/cpuinfo are *higher* than the actual cpu clock. that is, 4500 for my cpu running at 4000.

i've already had this issue with some tools under windows, so it's probably some lack of support with the pretty new wolfdale series.

### Post by GTengineer on 2008-04-06
> **roots said:**
> i know, but that's a different thing. i have speedstep disabled in bios for this kind of testing business. furthermore, the clockings shown by - for example - cat /proc/cpuinfo are *higher* than the actual cpu clock. that is, 4500 for my cpu running at 4000.

i've already had this issue with some tools under windows, so it's probably some lack of support with the pretty new wolfdale series.

do you have it set at a lower multipler with higher FSB?

For example when I run at 3.6GHz with 8x450 some tools like coretemp in Windows don't realize that my multiplier is lower so it thinks I am running 9x450 so it reports a wrong clock speed of 4050MHz.

---

### Post by roots on 2008-04-06
that's exactly the reason. standard multi of the e8500 is 9.5, so when i'm running 475x8.5=4037 it shows me 475x9.5=4513.

---

### Post by Lster on 2008-04-09
Out of interest I benchmarked my laptop again - with Hardy alpha. My last benchmark, with an older Hardy or Gutsy (I can't remember which), gave me a total time of:

> 17.637 seconds.

My latest test (on Hardy) gives me:

> 17.289 seconds.

The difference is small, but quite consistent; both times I ran my test I ran super_pi a couple of times and each time they were similar to previous results...

### Post by K.Mandla on 2008-04-17
Does anyone know if system load will have an effect on Super Pi results? I have two different machines that see longer total calculation times in X-less, terminal-only environments. I thought perhaps I was reading the results wrong. Thanks, if you can help.

---

### Post by Frak on 2008-04-19
> **K.Mandla said:**
> Does anyone know if system load will have an effect on Super Pi results? I have two different machines that see longer total calculation times in X-less, terminal-only environments. I thought perhaps I was reading the results wrong. Thanks, if you can help.
I've found that using yaourt in the background will hurt my results, so, yes, system load at least affects me. Best to just use a vc or tty to do it in.

---

### Post by Vadi on 2008-07-10
Guys... you do know that if you use the [Phoronix Test Suite]("http://www.phoronix-test-suite.com/") for the super pi benchmarks, you could compare your results with pretty graphs?

Download and install it - atm it's terminal only (but a GUI is being worked on, with the design by yours truly). After installing, simply paste this command:

> phoronix-test-suite benchmark vadi-12658-6005-31214

to compare your results against mine. Upload them onto PTS global too :)

PS. Oh and my test result is available online here ([click]("http://global.phoronix-test-suite.com/index.php?k=profile&u=vadi-12658-6005-31214")).

### Post by roots on 2008-09-20
if you're just looking for decent superpi scores, forget the quad. superpi utilizes only one core, unless you run two instances at the same time, which will calculate something like 6.283.... ;)

---

### Post by roots on 2008-09-20
> **bras said:**
> WOW thats fast :P and just aircooled

yup, the scythe andy samurai is a tough cooler. when i'm doing ordinary work without much cpu usage, i have the fan spin down completely and don't get any higher than 40 degree c at standard 3.2 ghz and freq scaling. however, 4.2 ghz is already pretty high, i could definitely up it even more, but that would involve raising vcore to spheres where it doesn't belong. 4.0 ghz is a safe limit for cpu heavy applications or games even over several hours.

---

### Post by BlueSkyNIS on 2008-09-20
> **roots said:**
> if you're just looking for decent superpi scores, forget the quad. superpi utilizes only one core, unless you run two instances at the same time, which will calculate something like 6.283.... ;)

No, super_pi means nothing to me. I need a quad CPU because I do a lot of video converting, and a quad would decrease processing time by a factor of 2 or more.

### Post by poisonkiller on 2008-09-20
I ran that program in Windows (Core2Duo E7200, 2.53GHz, 4GB RAM) and then in my Ubuntu virtual machine (VMware Workstation 6.5 Beta, single-core 2.53GHz, 1GB RAM), here are the results:
Windows: 21 seconds
Ubuntu: 19.229 seconds

I just don't get how it's possible... :D

### Post by IgnorantGuru on 2008-10-04
I just built a new dual-core system so I had to try this...

CPU: Intel Core 2 Duo E8500 Wolfdale 3.16GHz 6MB L2 Cache
Memory: DDR3 1333MHz

I tried running 1, 2, 3, and 4 calculation simultaneously with these results.  2 was indeed as fast as 1, and 4 was as fast as 2.

one simultaneous calculation: 11.7 sec
two simultaneous calculations: 13.0 sec
three simultaneous calculations: 19.7 sec
four simultaneous calculations: 26.5 sec

### Post by bgerlich on 2009-01-18
sad_iq, well that's because super pi is single threaded. What you are really comparing is your processor vs 1/4 of his proc ;)

### Post by OpenSourceRules on 2009-01-18

---

### Post by Spr0k3t on 2009-01-20
> **kernelhaxor said:**
> I didn't know linux kernel already supported x58 chipsets? .. Nice stats!

Thanks.  The x58 chipsets work, but I went with a board that has a solid reputation (Asus P6T, even though I'm not a fan of Asus) after doing a bit of research.  From what I understand, everything on the board works with the exception of the sensors and SAS.  If I could do SAS on that rig, I'd be using a 15K as my primary drive.  As it is, OOo apps launch about as fast gnome-terminal... I don't even see the splash screen.  Kind of cool seeing 8 cores show up in htop.

---

### Post by bgerlich on 2009-04-04
> **starfry said:**
> Do you know if this super_pi runs across all cores?
(there seems to be no help or man page)

SuperPi is single threaded, MultiPi works on multiple cores.

### Post by BillZwicky on 2009-06-25
The simple way to test multiple cores is to run one copy of pi for each core.  But you can't "just" run it twice, since they'll will both try to write to the same PI.DAT file, causing one to crash.  You need something like this:

```

mkdir 1
mkdir 2
(cd 1;../super_pi 20) & (cd 2;../super_pi 20)

```

You can compare the PI.DAT files when its done to be sure they all got the same answer.

This is a great way to stress your CPU, as I found out when I overclocked mine:  The above code has about a 66% chance of causing a kernel oops.  I'm not entirely certain that there isn't a bug in the kernel, but all signs point to the overclocking.

---

### Post by mu:te on 2009-08-26
WHY IN GODS WORLD DOES LIFE HAVE TO BE SO COMPLICATED SOMETIMES????

EASIEST GUIDE EVER!

1.Download
2.open a console window and enter the directory where super_pi.tar.gz is saved.
3.untar with "tar xvf super_pi.tar.gz"
4.enter the extract directory
5. run super pi with "sh super_pi 20"

WORKS FOR EVERYONE, BUT NOOOO NOT ME, OF COURSE..

> root@daaniel:/home/daaniel/Desktop# sh super_pi 20
super_pi: 1: ./pi: not found
root@daaniel:/home/daaniel/Desktop# sh pi 20
pi: 1: Syntax error: "(" unexpected
root@daaniel:/home/daaniel/Desktop# 


If I simply type "pi 20" I get "3.1415926535897932384" thats right, 20 digits of pi, not 1m nor no time..

### Post by hwttdz on 2009-08-26
Try running these commands:

cd ~
mkdir temp
cd temp
wget wget [http://www.di.uminho.pt/~apa/directorias/public/download/performance/super_pi.tar.gz](http://www.di.uminho.pt/~apa/directorias/public/download/performance/super_pi.tar.gz)
tar -zxf super_pi.tar.gz
./super_pi 20

It goes to your home directory, and makes a temp folder there, downloads superpi, extracts, and runs.

And:

### Post by mu:te on 2009-08-26
> **hwttdz said:**
> Try running these commands:

cd ~
mkdir temp
cd temp
wget wget [http://www.di.uminho.pt/~apa/directorias/public/download/performance/super_pi.tar.gz](http://www.di.uminho.pt/~apa/directorias/public/download/performance/super_pi.tar.gz)
tar -zxf super_pi.tar.gz
./super_pi 20

It goes to your home directory, and makes a temp folder there, downloads superpi, extracts, and runs.


Thanks for the help, although this stuff is so dope that it denies to work..

> daaniel@daaniel:~/temp$ ./super_pi 20
./super_pi: line 1: ./pi: No such file or directory


But to test your stability on the CPU you can download, LinX or Orthos. If you can run it for 2 hours you are fairly stable, if you run it for 24 hours your counted as 24/7 stable.

On LinX run the test with maximum possible memory but with orthos run Small FTTs. I would also recommend that you buy the program Everest, most accurate program you can get for watching the heat of your hardware, and also every single other information.

Also try one thing for me, since you can run Super pi. Type, the command you use to start the test, lets call it "X" and make it look like this "X & X & X & X", eg. "super_pi 20 & super_pi 20 & super_pi 20 & super_pi 20" Ive heard that this might run all your cores not just one, but could be some non-sens crap..

---

### Post by mgmiller on 2009-08-26
> Also try one thing for me, since you can run Super pi. Type, the command you use to start the test, lets call it "X" and make it look like this "X & X & X & X", eg. "super_pi 20 & super_pi 20 & super_pi 20 & super_pi 20" Ive heard that this might run all your cores not just one, but could be some non-sens crap..

I just tried it.  Only 1 out of 3 cores engaged and only 1 core out of 3 scaled back up to full speed.

So... It's "non-sens crap".  At least in my system.

---

### Post by hwttdz on 2009-08-26
Well the & will run the job in the background, so if you were to make a script:

./superpi 20 &
./superpi 20 &
./superpi 20 &
./superpi 20 &

It would run on all 4 cores, but because of the way superpi is written you're only going to use one core per process.  If it did run in parallel (or multithreaded?) then you would only have to submit one superpi job and it would use as many processors as available.  

As it is my cpus are busy for the next 36 hours (100% cpu usage all 4 cores, btw this is why overclocking is a godsend, that would be closer to 45 hours at stock speeds).  Granted superpi only runs for a few seconds, but I don't have any more questions about it.

---

### Post by mgmiller on 2009-08-26
If what you are saying is true, then it would still just be running a separate instance of super pi on each core.  

It would be more interesting to see how much faster it would run if a single instance of super pi could be run using all the cores together, multithreaded.

---

### Post by bcboybuzz on 2009-08-26
To think my faithful e8400 can still keep up with [especially with oc] an i7 makes me happy that I never wasted my money...Four separate runs, one in powersave state [2.0GHz] one in OnDemand state, one in performance state [3.0GHz] and finally one with the FSB at 400 instead of 333 [3.6GHz]. This is only half what it can be bumped up to before requiring water coolant systems. There's more than one confirmation on the web of getting stable 4.2 GHz, including my own rig...Notice that in OnDemand state, only 2 seconds were gained versus powersave, compared to six when CPU was forced to full power...

### Post by RabbitWho on 2009-09-08
> **coldReactive said:**
> 


Is that one GHz the thing that's making all the difference?

---

### Post by coldReactive on 2009-09-08
> **RabbitWho said:**
> Is that one GHz the thing that's making all the difference?

Faster you go, the less things need to be done over longer periods of time. If I upgrade to a quad core, a lot of things can be done without much strain on the CPU.

However, if I want to upgrade to a Quad Core that's 3 GHz, I have to upgrade my power supply.

---

### Post by moster on 2009-09-08
> **RabbitWho said:**
> Is that one GHz the thing that's making all the difference?

CPU frequency, FSB frequency and amount of cache. It all matters nearly equally.

---

### Post by lukjad on 2009-09-15
> **sapo said:**
> SuperPi is a quick and easy way to measure processor / chipset performance.

You can Download the linux version here:

[ftp://pi.super-computing.org/Linux/super_pi.tar.gz](ftp://pi.super-computing.org/Linux/super_pi.tar.gz)

Just untar and run the command as:

```
./super_pi 20
```

Then post a screenshot of your results or paste it  :-P 

I didnt know that there was a superpi for linux, i used to use it in windows to test my overclock.. my new cpu arrived today and i tested my first overlclock:

A64 2800+ @ 2ghz s754 + Mobo Epox 8KRDA3J Running Hoary x86

[img]http://img376.imageshack.us/img376/7343/superpi7dx.jpg[/img]

Please post your score  :grin:

### Post by mr clark25 on 2009-12-25
sorry for pasting so many times in a row.

i made a script that does the same thing as super pi (although it doesn't  time itself). but, you must have pie installed to run it.
[quote=]code:
sudo apt-get install pi[/quote]

install pi, then download the script, and run it. i think it runs faster than super pi, because i got 11.5 seconds on a dual core running at 1.8ghz. you may need to make it executable. there is no need to open a terminal to run it. you can do it graphicly. (double click, select option "run in terminal") at the end it may display a time, but its not the time it took to complete. so, you just need to time it yourself...

### Post by dbbolton on 2009-12-25
> **mr clark25 said:**
> sorry for pasting so many times in a row.

i made a script that does the same thing as super pi (although it doesn't  time itself). but, you must have pie installed to run it.


install pi, then download the script, and run it. i think it runs faster than super pi, because i got 11.5 seconds on a dual core running at 1.8ghz. you may need to make it executable. there is no need to open a terminal to run it. you can do it graphicly. (double click, select option "run in terminal") at the end it may display a time, but its not the time it took to complete. so, you just need to time it yourself...
Your script seems to be calling 'pi' more than once. Still, if it is faster than Super Pi, then its results are totally irrelevant to all others in this thread.

If you really want to use the 'pi' program in the repositories to calculate pi to 2^20 digits and time the process, all you need to run is this command:
```

time pi 1048576 | egrep '.*total$'

```And for me, running a T7600, this only took about 4 seconds. So I am pretty sure that this program isn't at all comparable to Super Pi.

The 'time' command is usually accurate to a few milliseconds:
```
time sleep 1
sleep 1  0.00s user 0.00s system 0% cpu 1.003 total

```

Considering human reaction time, I would guess that using a stop watch is going to be at least 1/3 of a second off, and probably way more for really short periods.

---

The 'time' command is usually accurate to a few milliseconds:
```
time sleep 1
sleep 1  0.00s user 0.00s system 0% cpu 1.003 total

```



Considering human reaction time, I would guess that using a stop watch is going to be at least 1/3 of a second off, and probably way more for really short periods.

```time is def accurate ... 
```

real    0m1.001s
user    0m0.000s
sys    0m0.000s
mark@mark-desktop:~$
```
If you are using bash, there is no need to grep for 'total' because bash will print it as 'real'. Of course it does suppress the number output of pi.

Then again, 'time' is pretty greedy. It will time whatever comes after it, including the pipe.

### Post by zeno0771 on 2010-02-02
> **powel212 said:**
> For some reason super pi doesn't run in Karmic.

P.

Karmic isn't the only one...

My main distro is actually Arch64 and the current super_pi binary segfaults there too.

FWIW I found something at Sourceforge which should do the trick and save some of us the headache of trying to get Phoronix Test Suite to play nice:

[http://systester.sourceforge.net/downloads.html]("http://systester.sourceforge.net/downloads.html")

This has a GUI as well as CLI and provides both algorithms, and you can run it from 128K to 128M decimal places.  What's more, there are versions for 32 and 64-bit Linux as well as Mac and Windoze.  Personally I think a common (and open-source :D) cross-platform app is the best way (if any of you tried taking your super_pi time over to a typical OC forum you know they'll usually reject your results), now there's no "unfair advantage" from some perceived difference in compiling for WinUsers to harp on.

### Post by Peacepunk on 2010-09-23
> **wmcbrine said:**
> I'll run this when it's open source.

You right!

**Learn to do this by hand!**

   ```
[SIZE="4"]echo 'scale=XXXX;4*a(1)' | bc -l[/SIZE]
```

where XXXX is the number of decimals to be calculated.

 >  **echo** = put on screen
 **scale** = the decimals
 **4*a(1)** = 4 times arctangent of 1
 **|** = pipe it trough...
 **bc** = the arbitrary precision calculator
 **-l** = using predefined math routines

Just add a date print before and after to know your time.

[tested on *Debian Squeeze* and *Arch*, sorry missus is away with 10.04 netbook. you may need to ***sudo apt-get install bc*** to do this]

from the Slackware Contributor of Fame AlienBob:
[http://alien.slackbook.org/blog/calculating-pi/](http://alien.slackbook.org/blog/calculating-pi/)

(Well, then, this whole 'my [SIZE="3"]&#960;[/SIZE] is shorter than yours' doesn't really impress me anyway.)

Cheers! Enjoy!

### Post by Objekt on 2010-12-22
Well, phooey!  I get the same error when trying to run Super Pi:
```
time pi 1048576 | egrep '.*total$'
```
Must be something Super Pi doesn't like about Ubuntu 10.04 and later.

Doing it the other way, however, I get:
```
user4@user4-desktop:~/$ time pi 1048576 | egrep '.*total$'

real	0m6.546s
user	0m6.452s
sys	0m0.096s

```
That's the result running it natively in Ubuntu 10.04 on the hardware listed in my sig.  But there seems to be a lot of variation.  Running it a minute or two later, I got:
```
user4@user4-desktop:~$ time pi 1048576 | egrep '.*total$'

real	0m5.239s
user	0m5.180s
sys	0m0.064s

```

Not sure what that means.

### Post by kapetr on 2011-06-19
Who has comm with the construction "egrep '.*total$'" ?!

There a no "total" lines - neither in time nor in pi command.

It is enough to run:

> hugo@duron650:~$ time pi 1048576 >/dev/null

real	0m11.626s
user	0m10.265s
sys	0m0.336s
hugo@duron650:~$ 


on my Sempron 2700+ (Socket A at 1.9Ghz)

BTW - There must be a great difference between SuperPI an PI.

e.g. Intel i3-530 takes 15s for SuperPI 1M - an I really don't thing, my CPU is faster :-)


--kapetr

### Post by Artemis3 on 2012-07-01
You should forget super pi and go with [Systester](http://systester.sourceforge.net/). super pi is not even multi threaded, so its results are meaningless.

Systester on the other hand can properly use all your cpu cores.
